_comment: lessons.json — thints language curriculum. Word data is pulled from dictionary.json at runtime using the IDs in wordIds. Only sentences, prompts, and structural metadata are hardcoded here.

units.title: Foundation
units.description: How the language is built — structure, the glue words, and your first sentences.

units.title: Time & Logic
units.description: Tense, time words, logical connectives, and how to modify and negate.

units.title: Vocabulary
units.description: Expanding your word bank across the core semantic groups.

units.title: Advanced Grammar
units.description: Modals, commands, subordinate clauses, evidentiality, and numbers.

units.title: Fluency
units.description: Putting it all together — complex sentences and borrowing words.

l01.title: How words are built
l01.subtitle: Suffixes, vowels, and the 𐑯𐑑 family
l01.description: Every word in thints is built from a suffix (the topic family) and a vowel (which specific idea). Learn to read any word before you know it.

grammar.title: Word structure: suffix + vowel
grammar.body:
Every regular word is built from two pieces: a suffix and a vowel.

The suffix tells you the topic family — all words ending in 𐑯𐑑 are about existence and state. The vowel tells you which specific idea within that family.

So 𐑩𐑯𐑑 (alive) and 𐑦𐑯𐑑 (life) are in the same family — they're both about existence — but different vowels pick different ideas.

grammar.title: 44 suffix families
grammar.body:
There are 44 suffix families in thints. When you hear an unfamiliar word, the suffix tells you the topic before you know the specific meaning.

Hear a word ending in 𐑯𐑑? You know it's about existence or state. Hear 𐑛𐑟? You know it's a positive emotion. This makes the language highly decodable — even new words give you a clue.

practice.prompt: What does the suffix 𐑯𐑑 tell you about a word?
options: It belongs to the existence & state family
practice.explanation: The suffix 𐑯𐑑 marks the existence & state family. All words with this suffix deal with being, living, or existing.

practice.prompt: Which part of 𐑩𐑯𐑑 tells you which specific idea it represents?
practice.explanation: The vowel 𐑩 picks the specific idea (alive/active) within the 𐑯𐑑 family. Different vowels pick different ideas in the same family.

practice.prompt: 𐑦𐑯𐑑 and 𐑩𐑯𐑑 share the same suffix 𐑯𐑑. What does that tell you?
options: They are both about existence and state
practice.explanation: Same suffix means same family. 𐑦𐑯𐑑 (life) and 𐑩𐑯𐑑 (alive) are both in the existence & state family — different vowels, different ideas, same topic.

practice.prompt: Match each word to its meaning
You see a word ending in 𐑛𐑟 that you have never seen before. What do you know about it before looking it up?
options: Nothing — you need to look it up
It is in the positive emotions family
It is a verb in past tense
practice.explanation: The 𐑛𐑟 suffix marks the positive emotions family. Knowing suffixes lets you make an educated guess about any unfamiliar word.

l02.title: The glue words
l02.subtitle: Single-vowel reserved words
l02.description: Fourteen single-vowel words do enormous work in thints. They never take a prefix or suffix — they are their own complete words.

grammar.title: Single-vowel reserved words
grammar.body:
These words are each a single Shavian vowel. They are reserved — they cannot be modified with prefixes or suffixes. They function as-is.

Some are pronouns (𐑰 = I, 𐑴 = you, 𐑱 = it/they, 𐑵 = we). Others are connectors (𐑧 = and, 𐑲 = but, 𐑨 = or). Others are positional (𐑩 = to/toward, 𐑫 = in, 𐑦 = if).

These are extremely high-frequency — ranks 2 through 15 in the dictionary. Mastering them gives you the skeleton of any sentence.

practice.prompt: What does 𐑰 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑰 is the first-person pronoun — I, me, myself, my.

practice.prompt: What does 𐑧 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑧 means and, plus, also — it joins things together.

practice.prompt: You want to say 'you or I'. Which is correct?
practice.explanation: 𐑨 means or / either. 𐑧 means and. 𐑲 means but. 𐑦 means if.

practice.prompt: Match each reserved word to its meaning
Can you add a prefix to 𐑰 to change how it is used?
options: Yes — prefixes apply to all words
No — single-vowel reserved words never take prefixes
Yes — but only in formal writing
practice.explanation: Single-vowel reserved words are fixed. They cannot be modified with prefixes or suffixes — they work exactly as they are.

l03.title: Nouns and subjects
l03.subtitle: The subject prefix 𐑐
l03.description: Every root word in thints is POS-neutral — it is neither a noun nor a verb until a prefix is added. The prefix 𐑐 marks the subject of a sentence.

grammar.title: Roots are POS-neutral
grammar.body:
In most languages, words have a fixed part of speech — 'run' is a verb, 'dog' is a noun. In thints, every root word is neutral. The prefix you attach determines whether it functions as a noun, verb, modifier, or something else.

This is one of the most important ideas in the language. The same root can play any role in a sentence depending on its prefix.

grammar.title: The subject prefix 𐑐
grammar.body:
𐑐 marks the subject — who or what performs the action.

Attach 𐑐 to any root and you have a subject noun. The prefix comes before the root with no space.

practice.prompt: What does the prefix 𐑐 mark?
practice.explanation: 𐑐 marks the subject — who or what performs the action.

practice.prompt: 𐑩𐑙 means 'someone'. How do you say 'someone (as the subject)'?
practice.explanation: Prefixes attach to the front of the root. 𐑐 + 𐑩𐑙 = 𐑐𐑩𐑙.

practice.prompt: A thints root word without a prefix is...
options: POS-neutral — its role is undefined until a prefix is added
practice.explanation: Roots are POS-neutral. They need a prefix to have a grammatical role in a sentence.

practice.prompt: Match the root to its meaning
How do you say 'a person (as the subject)'?
practice.explanation: 𐑐 (subject prefix) + 𐑧𐑙 (person) = 𐑐𐑧𐑙.

l04.title: Your first verbs
l04.subtitle: Present tense — prefix 𐑥
l04.description: The prefix 𐑥 marks a verb in present tense. Combined with the subject prefix from L3, you can now make your first real sentences.

grammar.title: Present tense: prefix 𐑥
grammar.body:
𐑥 marks a verb in present tense. The same root that was a subject noun with 𐑐 becomes a verb with 𐑥.

This is the power of the prefix system — the root stays the same, the prefix changes the role.

grammar.title: Word order is mostly free
grammar.body:
In thints, word order is flexible. The prefixes tell you each word's role — you don't need position to know what is the subject and what is the verb.

The one constraint: modifiers must be placed directly next to what they modify. You'll learn this when we reach modifiers.

practice.prompt: What does the prefix 𐑥 mark?
practice.explanation: 𐑥 marks a verb in present tense.

practice.prompt: How do you say 'knows' (present tense verb) using the root 𐑩𐑒𐑑?
practice.explanation: 𐑥 (present tense) + 𐑩𐑒𐑑 (know) = 𐑥𐑩𐑒𐑑. Prefixes always attach to the front.

practice.prompt: What is the difference between 𐑐𐑩𐑒𐑑 and 𐑥𐑩𐑒𐑑?
options: 𐑐𐑩𐑒𐑑 is past tense, 𐑥𐑩𐑒𐑑 is present tense
𐑐𐑩𐑒𐑑 is 'knowledge' as a subject noun, 𐑥𐑩𐑒𐑑 is 'knows' as a verb
𐑐𐑩𐑒𐑑 is formal, 𐑥𐑩𐑒𐑑 is informal
practice.explanation: Same root 𐑩𐑒𐑑 (knowledge/knowing), different prefixes. 𐑐 makes it a subject noun, 𐑥 makes it a present-tense verb.

practice.prompt: What does 𐑐𐑰 𐑥𐑩𐑐𐑕 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑐𐑰 = I (subject), 𐑥𐑩𐑐𐑕 = want (present verb). So: I want.

practice.prompt: Thints word order is...
options: Mostly free — prefixes tell you each word's role
practice.explanation: Word order is mostly free in thints. Because prefixes mark each word's grammatical role, position doesn't carry that information.

l05.title: Objects and indirect objects
l05.subtitle: Prefixes 𐑑 and 𐑒
l05.description: The same roots from L3 and L4 can now play new roles. 𐑑 marks the direct object, 𐑒 marks the indirect object.

grammar.title: Direct object: prefix 𐑑
grammar.body:
𐑑 marks the direct object — who or what receives the action directly.

All noun prefixes use plosive stop sounds: 𐑐 𐑑 𐑒 𐑔. If you mishear one for another you still know it's a noun — just the role may be unclear.

grammar.title: Indirect object: prefix 𐑒
grammar.body:
𐑒 marks the indirect object — who or what benefits from or receives the result of the action.

In English: 'I gave the dog a bone' — 'the dog' is the indirect object (receives the benefit), 'a bone' is the direct object (receives the action directly).

practice.prompt: 𐑑 marks the...
practice.explanation: 𐑑 marks the direct object — who or what receives the action directly.

practice.prompt: 𐑒 marks the...
practice.explanation: 𐑒 marks the indirect object — who or what benefits from the action.

practice.prompt: In 'I gave you this', which word is the indirect object?
practice.explanation: 'You' receives the benefit of the giving — that's the indirect object. 'This' is what was directly given — the direct object.

practice.prompt: What does 𐑑𐑩𐑙 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑑 (direct object prefix) + 𐑩𐑙 (someone) = 'someone' as the direct object.

practice.prompt: All noun prefixes (𐑐 𐑑 𐑒 𐑔) share which acoustic quality?
options: They are all plosive stop sounds
practice.explanation: All noun prefixes use plosive stop sounds. Mishearing one for another still signals 'noun' — you may not know the exact role but you know it's a noun.

l06.title: Negation
l06.subtitle: The operator 𐑕 and the word 𐑧𐑯𐑟
l06.description: Two ways to negate in thints: the operator 𐑕 stacks onto prefix + root, and 𐑧𐑯𐑟 is the standalone negation word.

grammar.title: The negation operator 𐑕
grammar.body:
𐑕 is an operator — it stacks onto a base prefix to add negation. It always comes first in the stack.

Stacking order: negation (𐑕) + base role (𐑐/𐑑/𐑒/𐑥…) + plural (𐑮 if applicable)

So 𐑕𐑐𐑩𐑙 means 'not someone (as subject)' — the person doing the action is specifically not that thing.

grammar.title: The negation word 𐑧𐑯𐑟
grammar.body:
𐑧𐑯𐑟 is a standalone word meaning 'not / no / negate'. It belongs to the Logic & Connectives family (suffix 𐑯𐑟).

Use 𐑧𐑯𐑟 when you want to negate a whole idea rather than a specific prefix. It is often more natural in spoken sentences.

practice.prompt: What does the operator 𐑕 do?
practice.explanation: 𐑕 is the negation operator. It stacks before a base prefix: 𐑕 + 𐑥 = negated present verb, 𐑕 + 𐑐 = negated subject.

practice.prompt: What does 𐑕𐑥𐑩𐑐𐑕 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑕 (negation) + 𐑥 (present) + 𐑩𐑐𐑕 (want) = does not want.

practice.prompt: What is the correct stacking order for a negated subject noun?
practice.explanation: Negation (𐑕) always comes first: 𐑕 + base prefix + root.

practice.prompt: Match the word to its meaning
What is the difference between 𐑕𐑐𐑩𐑙 and 𐑧𐑯𐑟 𐑐𐑩𐑙?
options: No difference — they mean the same thing
𐑕𐑐𐑩𐑙 specifically negates the subject role; 𐑧𐑯𐑟 𐑐𐑩𐑙 negates the idea more broadly
𐑕𐑐𐑩𐑙 is formal, 𐑧𐑯𐑟 𐑐𐑩𐑙 is informal
𐑕𐑐𐑩𐑙 is a verb form, 𐑧𐑯𐑟 𐑐𐑩𐑙 is a noun phrase
practice.explanation: 𐑕𐑐𐑩𐑙 uses the operator to negate the specific subject role. 𐑧𐑯𐑟 𐑐𐑩𐑙 uses the standalone negation word to broadly negate the phrase.

l07.title: Past and future
l07.subtitle: Prefixes 𐑯 and 𐑛
l07.description: Tense is marked entirely by the verb prefix. The root word never changes — only the prefix does.

grammar.title: The tense system: 𐑥 𐑯 𐑛
grammar.body:
Three prefixes cover all simple tenses:

𐑥 — present: I know
𐑯 — past: I knew
𐑛 — future: I will know

All three use voiced nasals and continuants, so even if you mishear one, you know it's a verb in some tense.

grammar.title: Negated future: 𐑛𐑮
grammar.body:
The future tense uses 𐑮 for negation rather than 𐑕. So 'will not' is 𐑛𐑮, not 𐑕𐑛.

This is one of the few irregular forms. In the future verb slot, 𐑮 is repurposed as negation instead of plural. Context (verb position in the sentence) disambiguates.

practice.prompt: Which prefix marks past tense?
practice.explanation: 𐑯 marks past tense. 𐑥 = present, 𐑛 = future, 𐑚 = ongoing/progressive.

practice.prompt: How do you say 'I will want'?
practice.explanation: 𐑛 (future) + 𐑩𐑐𐑕 (want) = 𐑛𐑩𐑐𐑕. With subject 𐑐𐑰 (I): 𐑐𐑰 𐑛𐑩𐑐𐑕.

practice.prompt: How do you negate a future tense verb?
practice.explanation: Future negation uses 𐑛𐑮 (not 𐑕𐑛). So 'will not know' = 𐑛𐑮𐑩𐑒𐑑.

practice.prompt: Match the verb form to its tense
What does 𐑐𐑴 𐑯𐑩𐑐𐑕 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑐𐑴 = you (subject). 𐑯𐑩𐑐𐑕 = wanted (𐑯 past + 𐑩𐑐𐑕 want). So: you wanted.

l08.title: Time words
l08.subtitle: The 𐑯𐑛 suffix family
l08.description: The 𐑯𐑛 suffix family covers time — when things happen, how long they last, and their sequence.

grammar.title: The 𐑯𐑛 time family
grammar.body:
All words ending in 𐑯𐑛 deal with time in the abstract — when something happens, how long it lasts, and sequencing. These are different from clock time (that comes in the Time — Measurement & Cycles group).

Time words often appear without prefixes when used as sentence-level markers.

practice.prompt: What suffix family covers abstract time concepts?
practice.explanation: 𐑯𐑛 is the time — abstract & sequence family.

practice.prompt: Match the time word to its meaning
What does 𐑨𐑯𐑛 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑨𐑯𐑛 means duration, period, or span — how long something lasts.

practice.prompt: What does 𐑰𐑯𐑛 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑰𐑯𐑛 means moment, instant, or a specific point in time.

practice.prompt: How would you say 'soon, I will speak'?
practice.explanation: 𐑲𐑯𐑛 (soon) + 𐑐𐑰 (I, subject) + 𐑛𐑦𐑯𐑑𐑕 (will speak, future). Word order is flexible, so 𐑲𐑯𐑛 can come first.

l09.title: Logic and connectives
l09.subtitle: The 𐑯𐑟 suffix family
l09.description: The 𐑯𐑟 family handles logical relationships — of, not, because, condition, and more.

grammar.title: The 𐑯𐑟 logic family
grammar.body:
The 𐑯𐑟 suffix family covers logical connectives — the words that link ideas. You already know 𐑧𐑯𐑟 (not) from L6. This family includes of/between, yet/still, condition, because, and then.

These words often appear without prefixes, serving as sentence-level connectors.

practice.prompt: Match the logic word to its meaning
What does 𐑲𐑯𐑟 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑲𐑯𐑟 means because, since, or for — it introduces a reason.

practice.prompt: What does 𐑰𐑯𐑟 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑰𐑯𐑟 means then, next, or but — it marks sequence or contrast.

practice.prompt: How would you say 'I know, but I do not want'?
practice.explanation: 𐑦𐑯𐑟 means yet / still / nevertheless — it introduces a contrast. 𐑐𐑰 𐑥𐑩𐑒𐑑 (I know) 𐑦𐑯𐑟 (yet) 𐑕𐑥𐑩𐑐𐑕 (do not want).

practice.prompt: 𐑩𐑯𐑟 (of / between / relating) is used to show...
practice.explanation: 𐑩𐑯𐑟 shows a logical relationship — belonging, connection, or relevance between two things.

l10.title: Modifiers
l10.subtitle: Prefix 𐑖 — adjectives and adverbs
l10.description: The prefix 𐑖 turns any root into a modifier. Whether it reads as an adjective or adverb depends entirely on what it sits next to.

grammar.title: The modifier prefix 𐑖
grammar.body:
𐑖 is the only modifier prefix. One prefix covers both adjectives and adverbs — position determines which reading applies.

𐑖 before a noun = adjective ('big dog')
𐑖 before a verb = adverb ('runs quickly')

The negated form is 𐑠, not 𐑕𐑖 — 𐑠 is its own dedicated sound for negated modifiers.

grammar.title: Modifier adjacency rule
grammar.body:
The one hard word-order rule in thints: modifiers must be placed directly next to the word they modify.

This is enforced because without it, 𐑖𐑦𐑥𐑟 could modify anything in the sentence and the meaning would be ambiguous.

practice.prompt: What prefix marks a modifier (adjective or adverb)?
practice.explanation: 𐑖 is the modifier prefix. Same prefix for both adjectives and adverbs — position determines which.

practice.prompt: 𐑖𐑦𐑥𐑟 placed before a noun reads as...
practice.explanation: 𐑖 before a noun = adjective. 𐑖 before a verb = adverb. Same word, position decides.

practice.prompt: How do you form a negated modifier (e.g. 'not big')?
practice.explanation: 𐑠 is the dedicated negated modifier prefix. It is not 𐑕 stacked onto 𐑖 — 𐑠 is its own distinct sound.

practice.prompt: Match the root to its meaning
Why must modifiers be adjacent to what they modify?
options: Without it, the modifier's target is ambiguous since word order is otherwise free
practice.explanation: Because thints word order is otherwise free, the only way to know what a modifier modifies is its physical position next to the target.

l11.title: Ongoing action
l11.subtitle: Progressive aspect — prefix 𐑚
l11.description: 𐑚 marks ongoing or continuous action. It covers the present progressive and is the most common aspect marker.

grammar.title: Progressive aspect: 𐑚
grammar.body:
𐑚 marks an ongoing or continuous action — 'I am knowing' / 'I am wanting'. It is the present progressive by default.

𐑚 alone covers the most common case. Past and future progressive use the ticked sounds (𐑮' and 𐑑') which you'll learn later.

examples.gloss: I am knowing / I am getting to know

practice.prompt: What does the prefix 𐑚 mark?
practice.explanation: 𐑚 marks progressive aspect — ongoing or continuous action.

practice.prompt: What is the difference between 𐑥𐑩𐑒𐑑 and 𐑚𐑩𐑒𐑑?
options: 𐑥𐑩𐑒𐑑 is 'knows' (simple present); 𐑚𐑩𐑒𐑑 is 'is knowing' (ongoing)
𐑥𐑩𐑒𐑑 is formal; 𐑚𐑩𐑒𐑑 is informal
𐑥𐑩𐑒𐑑 is past; 𐑚𐑩𐑒𐑑 is present
practice.explanation: 𐑥𐑩𐑒𐑑 = knows (simple present). 𐑚𐑩𐑒𐑑 = is knowing / in the process of knowing (progressive).

practice.prompt: How do you say 'I am not speaking'?
practice.explanation: 𐑚𐑮 is the negated progressive (not ongoing). 𐑚𐑮 + 𐑦𐑯𐑑𐑕 (speak) = 𐑚𐑮𐑦𐑯𐑑𐑕 (is not speaking).

practice.prompt: 𐑚 alone defaults to which tense?
practice.explanation: 𐑚 alone = present progressive ('I am doing'). Past and future progressive use ticked sounds covered later.

practice.prompt: What does 𐑐𐑩𐑙 𐑚𐑩𐑐𐑕 mean?
options: someone is wanting / someone is in a state of wanting
practice.explanation: 𐑚𐑩𐑐𐑕 = is wanting (ongoing present progressive). With 𐑐𐑩𐑙 (someone, subject): someone is wanting.

l12.title: Quantity and possession
l12.subtitle: Prefix 𐑣
l12.description: 𐑣 marks quantity (how many) when placed before a number, and possession (belonging to) when placed before a pronoun.

grammar.title: Prefix 𐑣: quantity and possession
grammar.body:
𐑣 does double duty:

Before a number: quantity — 𐑣3 𐑩𐑙 = 3 somethings
Before a pronoun: possession — 𐑣𐑰 𐑩𐑙 = my something

Context determines which reading applies — a digit after 𐑣 means quantity; a pronoun after 𐑣 means possession.

practice.prompt: What does 𐑣 + a pronoun express?
practice.explanation: 𐑣 before a pronoun marks possession. 𐑣𐑰 = my, 𐑣𐑴 = your, 𐑣𐑵 = our.

practice.prompt: How would you say 'your thing'?
practice.explanation: 𐑣𐑴 = your (𐑣 possession + 𐑴 you). 𐑣𐑴 𐑑𐑦𐑙 = your thing.

practice.prompt: What does 𐑧𐑒𐑕 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑧𐑒𐑕 = each, every, all — universal quantity.

practice.prompt: Match the quantity/logic word to its meaning
𐑣 before a number vs 𐑣 before a pronoun — what is the difference?
options: No difference — 𐑣 always means possession
Before a number = quantity; before a pronoun = possession
Before a number = possession; before a pronoun = quantity
practice.explanation: 𐑣 is context-sensitive: before a digit it means quantity, before a pronoun it means possession.

l13.title: Relating things
l13.subtitle: Relation & Causation — the 𐑥𐑐 family
l13.description: Words about how things relate to each other — belonging, similarity, cause, and result.

grammar.title: The 𐑥𐑐 relation family
grammar.body: The 𐑥𐑐 suffix family covers how things relate — of/belonging, similar/like, solution/means, and cause/result. These words are frequently used as connectors in complex sentences.

practice.prompt: Match the relation word to its meaning
What does 𐑦𐑥𐑐 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑦𐑥𐑐 = similar, like, comparable — it marks similarity or analogy.

practice.prompt: How would you say 'this is like that'?
practice.explanation: 𐑐𐑦𐑙 (this, subject) + 𐑥𐑦𐑥𐑐 (is similar, present verb using root 𐑦𐑥𐑐) + 𐑑𐑦𐑙 (that, object).

practice.prompt: 𐑩𐑥𐑐 expresses...
practice.explanation: 𐑩𐑥𐑐 covers belonging to, of, origin, and causation — the most general relation word.

practice.prompt: What does 𐑧𐑥𐑐 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑧𐑥𐑐 = solution, means, method — the way something is achieved.

l14.title: Quality and value
l14.subtitle: Evaluation & Morality — the 𐑑𐑕 family
l14.description: Words about quality, standards, and judgment — from ok to outstanding, from value to wrongness.

grammar.title: The 𐑑𐑕 evaluation family
grammar.body:
The 𐑑𐑕 suffix family covers evaluation and moral judgment — how good, how appropriate, how valuable something is.

This family runs from ok/acceptable through great/excellent. The vowel marks the degree and valence.

practice.prompt: Match the evaluation word to its meaning
What does 𐑰𐑑𐑕 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑰𐑑𐑕 = valuable, expensive, precious — high worth.

practice.prompt: How would you say 'this is great'?
practice.explanation: 𐑐𐑦𐑙 (this, subject) + 𐑥𐑧𐑑𐑕 (is great, present verb from root 𐑧𐑑𐑕).

practice.prompt: What does 𐑱𐑑𐑕 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑱𐑑𐑕 = fair, equal, just — balanced evaluation.

practice.prompt: 𐑨𐑑𐑕 means...
practice.explanation: 𐑨𐑑𐑕 = quality, standard, grade — the level or measure of something's worth.

l15.title: Positive emotions
l15.subtitle: The 𐑛𐑟 family
l15.description: All positive emotions end in 𐑛𐑟. From delight to love to happiness — recognizing this suffix means you always know you're in emotional territory.

practice.prompt: Match the emotion to its meaning
What does 𐑱𐑛𐑟 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑱𐑛𐑟 = cute, charming, lovely — appealing in a soft way.

practice.prompt: How would you say 'I am happy'?
practice.explanation: 𐑐𐑰 (I, subject) + 𐑥𐑦𐑛𐑟 (am happy, present verb from root 𐑦𐑛𐑟).

practice.prompt: What does 𐑰𐑛𐑟 mean?
practice.explanation: 𐑰𐑛𐑟 = sweet, pleasant, delightful.

practice.prompt: What does the 𐑛𐑟 suffix tell you about a word before you know which one it is?
practice.explanation: 𐑛𐑟 is the positive emotions suffix. Any word ending in 𐑛𐑟 is in that family.

l16.title: Negative emotions
l16.subtitle: Negative emotion words
l16.description: The counterparts to L15 — negative emotions and their vocabulary.

l17.title: Motion
l17.subtitle: Motion & Movement — the 𐑖𐑑 family
l17.description: Words about how things move — direction, speed, travel.

l18.title: Place and space
l18.subtitle: Place & Dwelling + Axes & Dimensions
l18.description: Spatial language — where things are and how they relate in space.

l19.title: The body
l19.subtitle: Body — Basic Parts
l19.description: Core vocabulary for the human body.

l20.title: Social interaction
l20.subtitle: Social Interaction vocabulary
l20.description: Greetings, farewells, and the words that make social exchange work.

l21.title: Work and effort
l21.subtitle: Work, Effort & Outcome vocabulary
l21.description: Doing things, trying, succeeding, and failing.

l22.title: Nature
l22.subtitle: Nature & Elements vocabulary
l22.description: The natural world — elements, living things, and natural phenomena.

l23.title: Can, should, would
l23.subtitle: Modal prefixes 𐑟 𐑡 𐑜
l23.description: Modals express ability, obligation, and conditionality. They use voiced obstruents — acoustically verb-adjacent but distinct.

grammar.title: Modal prefixes: 𐑟 𐑡 𐑜
grammar.body:
Three modal prefixes:

𐑟 — can / could (ability)
𐑡 — should / must (obligation)
𐑜 — would / conditional

Negating ability (𐑟) and obligation (𐑡) uses a negative modifier word before the modal, not a stacked 𐑕 prefix. Only conditional (𐑜) takes a stacked 𐑮: 𐑜𐑮 = would not.

practice.prompt: Which prefix means 'can / could' (ability)?
practice.explanation: 𐑟 = can / could. 𐑡 = should / must. 𐑜 = would / conditional.

practice.prompt: How do you say 'I would not know'?
practice.explanation: Conditional negation uses 𐑜𐑮, not 𐑕𐑜. 𐑜𐑮𐑩𐑒𐑑 = would not know.

practice.prompt: To say 'I cannot speak', you use...
options: A negative modifier word before 𐑟 + root
practice.explanation: Ability (𐑟) and obligation (𐑡) negate with a modifier word before them, not a stacked prefix. 𐑜 is the only modal that takes a stacked negation (𐑜𐑮).

practice.prompt: Match the modal to its meaning
What acoustic quality do modal prefixes 𐑟 𐑡 𐑜 share?
practice.explanation: Modal prefixes use voiced obstruents — acoustically verb-adjacent but distinct enough to be recognizable as modals.

l24.title: Commands
l24.subtitle: Do! and Do not! — prefixes 𐑗 and 𐑢
l24.description: The imperative — telling someone to do or not do something. Phonetically distinct from all other verb prefixes.

l25.title: Subordinate clauses
l25.subtitle: Clause verb prefix 𐑓
l25.description: How to embed one clause inside another — 'I know that you want...'

l26.title: Reported speech
l26.subtitle: The evidential 𐑯'
l26.description: Marking whether you witnessed something directly or are reporting what you heard.

l27.title: Was doing, will be doing
l27.subtitle: Ticked progressive: 𐑮' 𐑑' 𐑕'
l27.description: Extending the progressive to past and future tenses using ticked consonants.

l28.title: Numbers
l28.subtitle: Sequence markers 𐑤 𐑘 𐑝 𐑶 𐑬
l28.description: How numbers work in thints — sequence boundaries, decimals, fractions, and scale.

l29.title: Complex sentences
l29.subtitle: Putting it all together
l29.description: Combining everything — free word order, modifier adjacency, multiple clauses, all tenses.

l30.title: Borrowed words
l30.subtitle: Alias roots and 𐑔
l30.description: How to bring words from other languages into thints — alias roots, the generic noun prefix, and conversation handles.
